Supercut · creative playbook

protein powder

What the longest-running paid social video ads in this market keep doing — counted, with the footage to prove it.

2026-07-12 · 54 ads studied across 38 brands

How we picked these ads

Advertisers kill losing ads fast, so an ad still delivering after a month has earned a look. We searched 216 ads in this market — including its heaviest spenders, so the pool reflects where real budgets go, not just what got views — and kept the ones running 30+ days and seen in the last 30. An AI watched each surviving ad and answered a fixed set of questions about it; code then counted the answers. 5 ads that turned out to sell something else were rejected — they are listed at the bottom. No single ad is called a winner here. The findings are patterns that repeat across 54 ads from 38 different brands.

What the studied ads sell

protein powder15 of 54
whey protein5 of 54
vegan protein powder3 of 54
amino acids3 of 54
whey protein isolate3 of 54
meal replacement shake2 of 54
clear whey protein2 of 54
smoothie powder2 of 54
19 one-off segments19 of 54

Top takeaways

43 of 54Show the product within the first three seconds of the video.
40 of 54Prioritize a creator feel over polished brand graphics.
52 of 54Ensure the video makes sense even when the sound is muted.
16 of 19Use an energetic emotional tone to keep viewers engaged.
  1. Start with a bold claim and energy ugc problem-solution · 19 ads
  2. Build trust with full product demos creator testimonial · 15 ads
  3. Focus on high visual cut counts product demo · 8 ads
  4. Maintain high speed and visual hooks lifestyle montage · 6 ads
  5. Use bold claims on static frames still image with text · 4 ads
  6. What to test next
  7. What to double-check

Chapter 1 · ugc problem-solution

Start with a bold claim and energy

19 ads 17 brands 214 median days live 3 median cuts in first 10s 28s median length

Runs on Facebook (12) and TikTok (7).

This format uses creators to bridge the gap between a struggle and the protein solution. Most and run for 15 to 30 seconds.

The first three seconds, side by side

bold claim5 of 19
question4 of 19

visual hook3 of 19

How to shoot it

  • Open with an energetic emotional tone. (16 of 19 ads)
  • Use a bold claim or a direct question as your hook. (9 of 19 ads)
  • Show the product on screen within the first 3 seconds. (12 of 19 ads)
  • Include a full demonstration of the powder being used. (16 of 19 ads)
  • Aim for a video length between 15 and 30 seconds. (12 of 19 ads)

Examples that lasted

Serena Neel
Opens with: “So a lot of people are confused why I bought a hundred orange juices.” · 518 days live · 10.0M plays · watch the ad
hauskris
Opens with: “A lot of people say that their protein shake recipe tastes like a milkshake and then you try it and it doesn't.” · 400 days live · 2.7M plays · watch the ad
SmallerSam_PCOS
Opens with: “Hailey Bieber’s Strawberry Glazed Erewhon Smoothie +40g protein” · 218 days live · 2.6M plays · watch the ad

Watch out

Almost no one in this category offers a discount in the hook; only 1 out of 19 ads did this.

Real people talking to the camera helps validate the supplement quality. These ads generally move at a medium pace.

How to shoot it

  • Ensure the creator shows a full demo of the product. (10 of 15 ads)
  • Lean into an energetic creator-led feel. (12 of 15 ads)
  • Place the product on screen immediately. (14 of 15 ads)
  • Use a soft call to action like shop now. (8 of 15 ads)
  • Maintain a medium pacing throughout the cut. (13 of 15 ads)

Nearly every ad in this group works with the sound off, except for 2 out of 15.

Chapter 3 · product demo

Focus on high visual cut counts

This format relies on showing the physical property of the powder. These are the most fast-paced ads in the pool.

How to shoot it

  • Use at least 7 cuts in the first 10 seconds of video. (8 of 8 ads)
  • Feature a strong visual hook to start the ad. (4 of 8 ads)
  • Show a full demonstration of the mixing or texture. (6 of 8 ads)
  • Keep the emotional tone energetic. (7 of 8 ads)
  • Ensure the product is seen in the first 3 seconds. (6 of 8 ads)

Runs on Facebook (3) and TikTok (3).

These ads use quick cuts to show the product fitting into a busy day. Every single ad in this group uses fast pacing.

How to shoot it

  • Use fast pacing for the entire duration. (6 of 6 ads)
  • Open with a visual hook rather than speech. (5 of 6 ads)
  • Average about 9.5 cuts in the first 10 seconds. (6 of 6 ads)
  • Feature the product within the first 3 seconds. (5 of 6 ads)

Watch out

Chapter 5 · still image with text

Use bold claims on static frames

4 ads 3 brands 183.5 median days live 0 median cuts in first 10s 7s median length

This format lives on Facebook: 4 of 4 ads.

Only 4 ads fit this format — read this chapter as a hint, not a rule.

Simple graphics with text overlays that run for very short durations. These are exclusive to Facebook in this pool.

How to shoot it

  • Keep the total video length under 15 seconds. (4 of 4 ads)
  • Present the product on screen immediately. (4 of 4 ads)
  • Use a hard call to action for immediate shopping. (4 of 4 ads)
  • Use a bold claim as the primary text hook. (2 of 4 ads)

Opens with: “CREATIVE FLAVORS THAT NAIL IT EVERY TIME!” · 194 days live · 0 plays · watch the ad

Watch out

None of these ads feature a creator or person on screen.

What to test next

Offer-Led UGC Hook

Hypothesis
If we open a UGC video with a discount offer, we will see higher conversion rates.
Why this test
Only 1 out of 19 ads in the UGC problem-solution category currently uses an offer or discount hook.
Call it on
Return on Ad Spend
Shot notes
  • Creator holds the bag of protein.
  • Overlay text showing a large percentage off.
  • Creator speaks directly to the camera about the deal.
Risk
The offer might attract one-time buyers rather than long-term fans.
Draft script
Stop paying full price for your protein! Get 20 percent off right now. It mixes perfectly and tastes like a milkshake. Click shop now to save.

Visual Product Tease

Hypothesis
If we delay the product reveal to the 3-10 second window in a testimonial, we will increase view time.
Why this test
Currently, 14 out of 15 creator testimonials show the product in the first 3 seconds.
Call it on
Average View Time
Shot notes
  • Start with a close up of the creator's face.
  • Use a problematic kitchen scene first.
  • Reveal the protein tub at the 5 second mark.
Risk
Viewers might scroll past if they do not know what is being sold immediately.
Draft script
I used to hate my morning routine. Everything felt heavy and I had zero energy. Then I found this secret. This protein powder changed everything.

Fast-Paced Testimonial

Hypothesis
If we apply the fast pacing of lifestyle montages to a creator testimonial, we will increase engagement.
Why this test
13 out of 15 creator testimonials use medium pacing, while 6 out of 6 lifestyle montages use fast pacing.
Call it on
Click-Through Rate
Shot notes
  • Cut between the creator talking and close-ups of the powder.
  • Include at least 8 cuts in the first 10 seconds.
  • Keep the creator's energy extremely high.
Risk
The message might become blurred or hard to follow if the cuts are too frequent.
Draft script
Best protein ever. (Cut) Look at that texture. (Cut) 25 grams of protein. (Cut) No chalky aftertaste. (Cut) You need this in your gym bag today.

What to double-check

Everything above comes from an AI watching ads one at a time and code counting its answers. Here is where that process was noisy, and what it threw out.

Ads rejected as off-market (5)

The search also returned ads selling something else. They were dropped before any counting, so they do not touch the numbers above.

AdvertiserWhy it was dropped
Waterboyoff-market: sells "Sugar Boy, a hangover recovery and electrolyte supplement."
Kate Martinoff-market: sells "Drip Drop, a fast hydration powder mix."
Chaz McNelisoff-market: sells "DripDrop hydration powder packets for fast rehydration."
Metagenics ANZoff-market: sells "Magnesium supplements for stress relief and relaxation."
MDRN Remediesoff-market: sells "Moringa dietary supplement capsules by MDRN Remedies."

How reliable was the watching?

We measured the editing pace of every ad twice: once by the AI's eye, once by scene-detection software. The AI was off by 2.4 cuts on average, and within 2 cuts of the software on 63% of ads. The cut counts printed in the chapters are the software's, not the AI's.

Where a spoken hook could be checked against the ad library's own transcript (19 ads), the AI's quote matched 18 times and disagreed 1 time (ad f2acbfe8687de138601ee). The other 35 ads had on-screen-text hooks, no hook, or no transcript — nothing to check against.

8 of 54 ads were described by the AI as making no ask, yet the ad unit itself carries a purchase button (ads f66d9c73952b213626aca, f0c9fbd9dc6b06d624ebc, f42800576e2de20271fa0, f7abe59147eeb4aa56ab2, ff0b38aa61bfbb3e935db, fdc8b6f3ddcc4216a9cbb, b13c9ab4748e89cc7abb, afc8513033f430404a49). Read those "no ask" counts with that in mind: the AI judges the video, not the button under it.

The AI reported firm format labels for every ad in the pool.

Assumptions this report makes

  • "Still delivering" means the ad was seen in the ad library within the last 30 days. The data offers no direct "currently live" flag.
  • Days-running and play counts come from the ad library and are taken at face value.
  • A long-running ad earned study, but nothing here proves any single ad was profitable. Only the repeated patterns are conclusions.
  • Spoken quotes were transcribed by the AI from the audio; where the ad library had its own transcript, we cross-checked (see above).

Study ranking: ads are ordered for the example lists by a score of longevity ×0.5 + engagement ×0.3 + formatConfidence ×0.2, each part normalized within this pool, with at most one ad per brand in any list. Engagement is measured as a rate — likes and shares per play, a share counting 5× a like — because plays on a paid ad are bought, so raw counts would mostly rank budgets. Rates from under 10,000 plays are ignored as noise.

Made with Supercut. Days-running and play counts come from the ad library data; "still delivering" means seen in the last 30 days, the closest signal the data offers to "currently live".
